We are here to lock the date and leave with an owner for each region.
Carla, you run that corridor, what is your read?
That is a good point, let us park it as a sidebar and come back to it.
In the interest of time, can we come back to that after?
Let me recap the decisions and owners before we adjourn.
Thanks for joining, everyone. We are here to lock the rollout date and leave with one owner per region, three items on the agenda, ten minutes each. Shall we start with the timeline?
Before that, Victor, we really need to talk about the budget cuts, because honestly none of this matters if my region loses two headcount next quarter.
That is a real concern, Beatriz, and I do not want to lose it. Would you mind parking it as a sidebar? Let us put budget right after this call so it gets proper time, and keep this hour on the date.
Okay, fair, as long as it actually happens and not next time. Put it on the sidebar list and I will hold it there.

Done, it is on the list. Now, on the date, Carla has been quiet and she runs the busiest corridor. Carla, what is your read on the fifteenth? And in the interest of time, could we keep it to the headline?
Carla just dropped off, but she sent me her note: the fifteenth works if her team gets the new vans by the tenth. So the date holds, with that one condition.

Perfect. Let me recap before we adjourn: rollout date is the fifteenth, conditional on Carla vans by the tenth, which I will own. Beatriz owns the budget sidebar right after this. Anything I have missed?
Nothing missed, and we finished on time, for once. That is the first review this year that ended with an actual decision.
I convened the leads, ran the agenda, and once we had quorum we decided.
When they digressed I parked it as a sidebar, steered us back, recapped the decisions, and adjourned on time.
Why do not we hear from Carla, what is your read?
Would you mind parking that as a sidebar for after?
In the interest of time, could we come back to that?

Right, let me think through how I chair this one before I walk in, because last quarter the cross-regional call ran forty minutes over and we left with no decision. Not again. First, I open with the purpose, not small talk. One line: we are here to lock the rollout date, and we leave with an owner for each region. That frames the whole hour. Second, I run the agenda visibly. Three items, a time box on each, and I say it out loud so the room polices itself. If we have quorum, we decide; if we do not, we note it and move on. Third, I manage the floor like a job, because it is one. The two loud ones will eat the hour if I let them, so when one digresses I park it: that is a good point, let us put it in a sidebar and come back to it. And the quiet regional leads always have the real information, so I bring them in by name: Carla, you run that corridor, what is your read? Fourth, I protect the time. The moment we drift, I say, in the interest of time, can we come back to that after, and I steer us back to the decision. And I never let it end loose. I close with a recap: here is what we decided, here is who owns what, here is the date. Then I adjourn on time. Purpose up front, agenda with time boxes, bring the quiet in and park the loud, guard the clock, recap and adjourn. That is chairing, not just attending.

Hi Victor, it's Beatriz. Quick voice note before you chair the cross-regional review, because running a room is a completely different muscle from being good in one, and most strong managers never learn it. Three things. First, the chair owns the purpose and the clock, nobody else. Open with why we are here and what a finished meeting looks like, put a time box on every item, and say it out loud so the group keeps itself honest. A meeting with no stated purpose drifts every single time. Second, your real job is the floor, not the content. The confident voices will dominate and the people with the actual answers will stay silent, so steer deliberately: park a digression instead of fighting it, push a side conversation into a sidebar for later, and bring the quiet ones in by name with a direct, easy question. You are not being rude, you are doing the job. Third, never let a meeting dissolve. Before anyone leaves, recap the decisions, name an owner for each action, confirm the date, and adjourn cleanly. If people walk out unsure what was decided, the meeting did not happen. Own the purpose, guard the clock, run the floor, and close with a recap and owners. Do that and people will actually want you in the chair.
